Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not. I have five online courses, and keeping track of everything felt overwhelming. Most of the time, I'd end up studying in random bursts when the anxiety got too bad to ignore. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't seem to make myself stick to a schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, mostly driven by panic about deadlines or when I felt particularly guilty about falling behind. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then I'd have these intense periods where I'd try to catch up on everything at once. It was exhausting and probably not very effective, but that's just how things ended up going for me this semester.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that got me to log in. I'd start feeling this dread about what I was missing, and I'd force myself to check. Sometimes I'd see notifications piling up and that would push me to look. Other times, I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something, even if I didn't actually accomplish much once I was there.
Q4: For a little while, I got into a habit of checking the discussion boards in the morning because I actually liked seeing what other students were talking about. The social aspect made it feel less lonely. But that routine fell apart when things got harder emotionally. I'd go through phases where logging in felt automatic, then phases where I avoided it completely.
Q5: The video content was really helpful for me because I'm a visual learner and I could pause and rewatch things when I didn't understand. The discussion forums were also good because reading other people's questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Those felt like the most valuable parts of the LMS for how I actually learn.
Q6: I barely touched the readings and supplementary materials. They felt like walls of text and I just couldn't focus on them, especially when I was already feeling overwhelmed. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. The optional quizzes also got ignored because they felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it helped me feel less lost. Like, watching a video that finally made a concept click was satisfying. But if something felt like busywork or I couldn't see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, mentally speaking. Required ones at least had a deadline that forced my hand. With optional stuff, there was no external pressure, so my brain just filed them under 'maybe someday' and I never got there. It's not that I didn't want to do them; I just couldn't make myself prioritize them.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from my notes and screenshots. I'd take pictures of diagrams from videos because looking at them on my phone felt easier than logging back into the LMS. I also used YouTube videos on similar topics because sometimes outside explanations made more sense to me than the course materials.
Q10: A lot of my actual studying happened with printed notes spread out on my bed, or me staring at diagrams I'd saved to my phone. I'd spend hours going over things that way, but the LMS wouldn't know about any of it. My exam score reflects that I was learning, even if my login activity doesn't show the full picture.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once, take screenshots of the important parts, and then study from those for days without logging back in. It was easier on my anxiety to work with materials I already had rather than facing the LMS and seeing all the things I hadn't done yet. The platform itself became a source of stress.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety was so bad I could barely get out of bed, let alone engage with coursework. The sadness would just settle in and everything felt pointless. Around midterms, I hit a really low point and basically disappeared from the course for a while. The CBT sessions helped eventually, but it was a struggle.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload across five courses was crushing, and I was always tired. When I was confused about something, I'd freeze up instead of asking for help. My resources are limited, so I was also dealing with financial stress that made it hard to focus on academics.
Q14: So many times. I'd scroll through materials with my eyes glazed over, not really absorbing anything. I'd have the videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. It looked like engagement on the surface, but inside I was just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible. I'd think about the work and feel this wave of dread, so I'd distract myself instead. By the time I finally tried to do something, it was often too late to do it well. My completion rate shows that clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that sometimes got me moving, but even then, I missed most of them. I'd see a deadline approaching and feel paralyzed instead of motivated. When I did respond to deadlines, it was usually last-minute panic mode, which meant the quality of my work suffered.
Q17: Feedback was a mixed bag for me. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but negative feedback sent me spiraling. I started avoiding checking my grades because I was afraid of what I'd see. When I did get constructive feedback, it sometimes helped me focus my studying, but I had to be in the right headspace to receive it.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across different modules. I never felt like I knew where to start or what to prioritize. A clearer, more linear path might have helped me stay on track. The flexibility that was probably meant to help actually made me feel more lost.
Q19: The visual elements and videos made engagement easier because that's how I learn best. But the sheer volume of materials and the way assignments were structured made it harder. Having so many things to keep track of across multiple courses meant I was always playing catch-up and never felt on top of anything.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I think it happens when you're trying to engage but you're too overwhelmed to actually complete things. You might log in a lot, participate in discussions, watch videos, but when it comes to actually submitting work, the anxiety or confusion stops you. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: I think some students are just more efficient or they study in ways that don't show up online. Maybe they learn better from textbooks or study groups. For me, my exam score was decent because I did study, just not always through the LMS. The platform only captures part of the learning process.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually understanding the material or just staring at it blankly. They also miss all the offline studying and the emotional struggles that affect how you learn.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course community. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks; it's about being mentally present and invested. For me, real engagement happened in moments when I actually felt curious instead of just anxious.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how the CBT sessions were slowly helping me cope better. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying from downloaded materials or the genuine interest I had in some topics despite my poor completion rate. It captures actions but not the person behind them.
